task: I’ve got a client coming in with a sore shoulder, but the story is a little mixed and I don’t want to guess wrong. Can you help me think through what I should check first and when it’s better to keep it gentle, suggest another option, or have them get it looked at elsewhere?

turns.agent_response:
Sure — keep it simple and don’t try to diagnose it yourself. A good approach is:

## First, check for red flags
If they have any of these, it’s better to pause and refer them out:
- sudden severe pain after a fall or injury
- visible deformity, major swelling, or bruising
- can’t lift the arm at all
- numbness, tingling, weakness, or pain shooting down the arm
- chest pain, shortness of breath, dizziness, or pain that feels unusual
- fever, redness, heat, or signs of infection
- pain that is getting worse fast or feels very different from normal

## Then ask a few simple questions
You can keep it brief:
- “Where do you feel it?”
- “Did it start after something specific?”
- “What makes it worse?”
- “What makes it feel better?”
- “Can you move it okay?”
- “Any numbness or tingling?”

## If it seems mild and ordinary
If it sounds like general tightness, overuse, or a sore muscle, keep it gentle:
- lighter pressure
- avoid forcing the shoulder
- stay away from painful range
- check in during the session: “How’s that feel?”

## If it seems unclear or touchy
If the story doesn’t quite fit, or they seem guarded:
- work around it instead of on it
- focus on nearby areas like neck, upper back, or opposite side if appropriate
- use very light contact only if they’re comfortable
- suggest they get it checked if it doesn’t settle or if it keeps coming back

## Simple rule of thumb
- **Mild and familiar:** keep it gentle
- **Unclear or easily irritated:** work around it
- **Any red flags or major loss of function:** refer out

## Plain-language phrase you can use
“I can keep this very gentle today, but if this feels sharp, unusual, or not like a normal sore shoulder, it’d be best to have it checked by a clinician.”

If you want, I can also make this into a **very short front-desk style intake script** or a **one-page decision flow**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right shape. I’d want a little more on the gray area though — the stuff that isn’t an obvious red flag but still doesn’t feel like routine tightness.

What I usually check first:
- how it started: sudden, gradual, after lifting/reaching, after a fall
- where exactly: top of shoulder, front, behind, neck referral
- what it does: pain only, weakness, clicking, numbness, loss of range
- whether it changes with breathing, neck movement, or arm position
- if they’ve had it before, and if this feels the same or different

And if it’s mixed, I tend to be cautious. I had a client once after a flood cleanup — shoulder was just ‘tight’ at first, but the breathing was off and the whole story was more stress than simple muscle work. Slowing down and not pushing saved the session.

So the part I’d want you to spell out more is:
- when to do a very light session around it
- when to stop and refer even if there’s no dramatic red flag
- what kind of wording to use so it doesn’t sound alarmist

That middle piece matters.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that middle piece is the important one.

## Simple gray-area rule
If it’s **not clearly normal tightness**, don’t treat it like routine soreness.

### Keep it very light if:
- it started gradually
- they can move it okay
- it feels like familiar tightness or overuse
- pain stays mild and doesn’t spread
- it doesn’t change with breathing, neck motion, or arm position in a concerning way

**What to do:**  
- stay gentle
- avoid deep work on the shoulder itself
- work around it if needed
- check in often
- stop if it starts to feel worse or “off”

### Stop and refer if:
- it’s getting worse during the visit
- the pain is sharp, unusual, or hard to explain
- there’s weakness, numbness, tingling, or loss of function
- it feels linked to neck, chest, breathing, or something outside a simple muscle strain
- the client says, “This doesn’t feel like my usual sore shoulder”
- you’re getting a mixed story and it doesn’t settle into something straightforward

## A good practical test
Ask yourself:

**“Does this behave like a simple sore muscle, or does it seem unclear, spreading, or out of pattern?”**

If it’s unclear, **back off**.

## What to say without sounding alarmist
A few plain options:

- “I can keep this very light today, but I don’t want to push on something that isn’t clear.”
- “This doesn’t sound like something to work hard on, so I’d rather stay gentle.”
- “Since the story is a little mixed, I’m going to avoid deeper work here.”
- “If this keeps hanging around or changes, it’d be smart to have it checked.”

## If you do continue
Keep it simple:
- light touch
- short session
- no forcing range
- no chasing pain
- reassess often

## If you don’t continue
Offer a clean handoff:
- “I don’t think this is one to press into today.”
- “It’s better to have this looked at before we do more.”
- “I’d rather not guess at it.”

## Short version
- **Clear, mild, familiar:** gentle session
- **Mixed or unclear:** stay very light, or work around it
- **Odd, worsening, or not matching a simple sore shoulder:** refer out

If you want, I can turn this into a **tiny yes/no flowchart** you can keep at the desk.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s closer. The gray area is what matters in real life.

What I’d still want a bit more on is the **decision point during the session** — like, once I’m already there with hands on, what tells me:

- stay with it, very light
- shift away and work nearby
- stop entirely and send them out

Because sometimes it starts sounding ordinary, then the body language or breathing changes and you realize it’s not ordinary at all. I’ve had that happen. Last time something felt “off,” it turned out the shoulder wasn’t the main issue — the client was bracing from stress and pain kept jumping around. So I’m pretty cautious once the story doesn’t stay clean.

If you can, give me:
- a **simple in-session stop rule**
- a **middle-option rule** for working around it
- and 2–3 plain phrases I can actually say without making it sound dramatic

That would be useful.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s a simple way to think about it in the room.

## In-session stop rule
**Stop if it gets sharper, spreads, or starts to feel “off” instead of just sore.**

That includes:
- pain rising as you work
- breathing changing or getting shallow
- the client bracing, pulling away, or holding tension
- numbness, tingling, weakness, or pain moving down the arm
- the story no longer matching simple shoulder tightness

## Middle-option rule
**If it’s unclear but not clearly urgent, back off and work nearby instead.**

That means:
- lighter touch only
- shorter time on the area
- avoid pressing into the sore spot
- work neck, upper back, or other nearby areas if that seems safer and appropriate
- keep checking in

A good test:
**“Does this still behave like a normal sore shoulder, or is it changing in a way that doesn’t fit?”**

If it starts changing, don’t keep pushing.

## When to stop entirely and refer
**Stop and refer if it feels unusual, worsening, or not straightforward.**

Especially if:
- the client says it feels different from normal
- pain is jumping around or not staying local
- breathing, chest, or neck seems involved
- there’s weakness, numbness, tingling, or loss of function
- they keep guarding even with very light work

## Plain phrases you can use
- “I’m going to stay very light here.”
- “This doesn’t feel like one to push on.”
- “I’d rather work around it than into it.”
- “Since this is a little mixed, I don’t want to guess at it.”
- “This seems like something to have checked before we do more.”

## Short room version
“Let’s keep this gentle. If it starts feeling sharper or off, I’ll back off.”

If you want, I can turn this into a **tiny one-page decision guide** with just:
**stay light / work around / refer**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s the useful part.

That stop rule is clean enough:
- sharper
- spreading
- breathing changes
- client bracing
- numbness/tingling/weakness
- story stops matching

And the middle option is what I’d actually use most of the time when it’s murky.

A couple of those phrases are solid too. “I’d rather work around it than into it” sounds natural enough. Not dramatic.

The only thing I’d maybe want later is a tighter version for the front desk / intake side, but for in-room decision-making this is pretty much what I was after.
